wheres = Vec::new();\n\n        if let Some(status) = filter.status {\n            wheres.push(\"status = ?\".to_string());\n            params.push(status.to_string());\n        }\n\n        if !wheres.is_empty() {\n            sql.push_str(\" WHERE \");\n            sql.push_str(&wheres.join(\" AND \"));\n        }\n\n        sql.push_str(\" ORDER BY created_at DESC\");\n\n        if let Some(limit) = filter.limit {\n            sql.push_str(&format!(\" LIMIT {limit}\"));\n        }\n\n        if let Some(offset) = filter.offset {\n            sql.push_str(&format!(\" OFFSET {offset}\"));\n        }\n\n        let params_refs: Vec<&dyn rusqlite::ToSql> =\n            params.iter().map(|s| s as &dyn rusqlite::ToSql).collect();\n\n        self.query_map(&sql, &params_refs, rusqlite::Error::ToSqlConversionFailure(Box::new(e)))?;\n            Ok(Task {\n                miroir_id: row.get(0)?,\n                created_at: row.get(1)?,\n                status: row.get::<_, The SQLite code uses `index` as a column name, which is a reserved keyword in SQL. The code has `index` (without quotes) which causes Rust string parsing issues when embedded in the SQL strings.\n\nLooking at the errors, the problem is that in several places, the code uses `\"index\"` inside the SQL strings (with the quotes being part of the string literal), but because the string is already using double quotes in Rust, this creates a conflict.\n\nThe proper fix is to escape `index` properly for SQLite. In SQLite, you can escape identifiers using:\n1. Double quotes: `\"index\"` \n2. Square brackets: `[index]`\n3. Backticks: `` `index` ``\n\nThe problem is that when you're writing a Rust string literal like `\"SELECT ... FROM index\"`, and you need to quote `index`, you can't just write `\"SELECT ... The code in many places uses the literal word `index` in SQL strings without properly escaping it for SQL.\n\nLooking at the clippy errors more closely, the actual issue is that in some places, the code is using `\"index\"` (with double quotes inside a double-quoted string), which creates invalid Rust syntax.\n\nFor example:\n```rust\n\"SELECT version FROM node_settings_version WHERE \"index\" = ?1 AND node_id = ?2\"\n```\n\nThis is invalid because the inner double quotes close the outer string. In SQLite, identifiers are escaped using double quotes, so we need to use:\n1. Raw string literals in Rust: `r#\"SELECT ... WHERE \"index\" = ?1\"#`\n2. Or escape the quotes: `\"SELECT ... WHERE \\\"index\\\" = ?1\"`\n3. Or use backticks for SQL escaping (SQLite specific): `` SELECT ... WHERE `index` = ?1 ``\n\nLet me look at the schema to understand what columns exist, then fix all the affected places.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":0},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"5d7bc4cd-0049-409a-a460-487797c96acd","uuid":"0cb80b3b-305c-4e90-a069-08f9758de012"}
